Article.ArticleTitle: N-terminal CBM3 fusion: A key factor for designing a stable cellulose-specific binding lactate oxidase sensing element.

Abstract.AbstractText: L-lactic acid is a key biomarker in clinical diagnostics, exercise physiology, and the food industry, driving demand for reliable detection methods. Electrochemical biosensors based on L-lactate oxidase (L-LOD) offer significant advantages, but their performance critically depends on effective enzyme immobilization techniques. This study developed a novel biosensing platform enabling one-step directed immobilization on cellulose membranes by constructing a fusion enzyme of L-LOD with the carbohydrate-binding module (CBM3). Among seven designed constructs by integrating CBM1, CBM2, or CBM3 at the N-/C-terminus of LOD (with/without linkers), only the N-terminal fusions, CBM3-LOD and CBM3-Linker-LOD, were successfully expressed and retained catalytic activity. Computational analyses revealed that the failure of other fusions (e.g., LOD-CBM2, CBM2-Linker-LOD) was due to steric hindrance and critical residue loss blocking the substrate access pathway. The resulting immobilized CBM3-LOD biosensor exhibited exceptional performance, demonstrating a wide linear range (0.0625-0.5 mM), a remarkably low detection limit of 0.00296 mM, high sensitivity (26.48 μA.mM-1.cm-2), and outstanding operational stability (>50% activity retained after 15 days). Real sample analysis in serum confirmed high accuracy (99.4-100.53% recovery) and excellent anti-interference ability. This work established CBM3-mediated immobilization as a superior strategy for developing high-performance biosensors and provided a rational design framework for engineering functional fusion multimeric enzymes.
